coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in carbon-based electrocatalysts for vanadium redox flow battery: Mechanisms, properties, and perspectives
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Qingchun
Ren, Yujie
Yang, Yujie
Wang, Ling
Dai, Lei
He, Zhangxing
coredata.dc:description: Vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) has the advantages of long life, high energy efficiency, safety, and environmental protection, so it has become an excellent energy storage system today. Electrode provides place for redox reactions, which has a significant impact on the performance of VRFB. At present, carbon materials are the most popular electrode materials for VRFB. However, original carbon materials have some problems such as poor electrochemical activity and hydrophilic properties, so they need to be properly modified before use. The introduction of carbon-based catalysts is an excellent way to improve electrode performance. In this paper, the latest progress of carbon-based materials as VRFB electrocatalysts was reviewed, including fullerenes, carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, graphene, biomass carbon, carbon-carbon composite, and other carbon materials. At the same time, modification methods, catalytic mechanism, and electrochemical performance of different carbon-based electrocatalysts were discussed. Finally, applications and challenges of carbon-based materials in VRFB was summarized, and some suggestions for the future development of carbon-based catalysts were put forward.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: At present, the preparation of carbon-based catalysts using polymers as precursors has been developed. Such catalysts usually have large specific surface areas, porous structures, and abundant heteroatoms. Shao et al. [118] prepared mesoporous carbon (MPC) by soft templating method using polymer as precursor, and then further processed in NH3 atmosphere to obtain nitrogen-doped mesoporous carbon (N-MPC). Nitrogen species lower the reaction energy barrier and the contact resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. And the pore size of N-MPC is only a few nanometers, which is beneficial to the transport of active species. Zhou et al. [47] used p-phenylenediamine and phytic acid as raw materials to obtain carbon network in situ modified GF (GF@CN) through cross-linking polymerization and carbonization (Fig. 12d). The CN supported on the GF surface provides a flow channel for the electrolyte (Fig. 12e). The presence of nitrogen and phosphorus species and oxygen groups in GF@CN reduces the activation energy for the redox reaction of vanadium ions (Fig. 12f). Compared with pristine GF electrode, GF@CN electrode accelerates the electron transfer rate and promotes the diffusion of active species. There is no degradation in the efficiency after 1000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 200 mA cm−2, which means that GF@CN electrode exhibits good cycling stability. Overall, heteroatom-doped carbon materials with abundant mesopores are a class of highly efficient electrocatalysts for VRFB. Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) is a promising precursor material with continuous microporous network. Kim et al. [119] treated PIMs with vapor induced phase separation technology and prepared GF (PIMC@GF) modified by nitrogen-doped graded porous carbon layer. Although there is no binder, PIMC can distribute uniformly and stably on GF surface and form defect structure. This solves the problem of large ohm resistance caused by the binder. Furthermore, the hierarchical pore structure, good wettability, and nitrogen species of PIMCs reduce mass transfer resistance and enhance redox activity.This work was ﬁnancially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51872090, 51772097), Hebei Natural Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholar (Grant No. E2019209433), Youth Talent Program of Hebei Provincial Education Department (Grant No. BJ2018020), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Grant No. E2020209151), Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (Grant No. SLRC2019028).

This work was ﬁnancially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51872090 , 51772097 ), Hebei Natural Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholar (Grant No. E2019209433 ), Youth Talent Program of Hebei Provincial Education Department (Grant No. BJ2018020 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Grant No. E2020209151 ), Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (Grant No. SLRC2019028 ).

head.ce:title: Recent advances in carbon-based electrocatalysts for vanadium redox flow battery: Mechanisms, properties, and perspectives

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, 063009, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan, 063009, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) has the advantages of long life, high energy efficiency, safety, and environmental protection, so it has become an excellent energy storage system today. Electrode provides place for redox reactions, which has a significant impact on the performance of VRFB. At present, carbon materials are the most popular electrode materials for VRFB. However, original carbon materials have some problems such as poor electrochemical activity and hydrophilic properties, so they need to be properly modified before use. The introduction of carbon-based catalysts is an excellent way to improve electrode performance. In this paper, the latest progress of carbon-based materials as VRFB electrocatalysts was reviewed, including fullerenes, carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, graphene, biomass carbon, carbon-carbon composite, and other carbon materials. At the same time, modification methods, catalytic mechanism, and electrochemical performance of different carbon-based electrocatalysts were discussed. Finally, applications and challenges of carbon-based materials in VRFB was summarized, and some suggestions for the future development of carbon-based catalysts were put forward.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of society, fossil energy is widely used. It leads to the reduction of fossil energy reserves and the serious pollution of the natural environment, which has a huge impact on people's life and health [1–3]. Therefore, renewable clean energy has become the focus of attention, such as wind, solar and tidal energy [4–6]. In order to make more effective use of these energy sources, redox flow battery (RFB) as an energy storage system has been widely studied and developed [7–11]. Among many RFB, vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) has become one of the most potential energy storage equipment because of its long life and low cost. In 1985, the VRFB concept was proposed by Skyllas-Kazacos M team [12]. After that, the team conducted in-depth studies and experiments on the reaction mechanism and electrode materials of VRFB, which made significant contributions to the development of VRFB in the future [13–16].

The structure diagram of VRFB is shown in Fig. 1 . VRFB is mainly composed of three parts: membrane, electrode, and electrolyte [17,18]. Membrane has two functions: on the one hand, it prevents the active material from crossing by separating the positive and negative electrolytes. On the other hand, it is responsible for the transmission of non-electrochemically active substances [19]. Electrode only provides a reaction site for the redox reaction and does not participate in the electrode reaction [20–22]. Positive and negative active substances are composed of VO2 +/VO2+ and V3+/V2+ pairs, respectively [23,24]. VRFB realizes the mutual conversion of chemical energy and electrical energy through the redox reaction between vanadium ions with different valence states. In the process of battery charging and discharging, the reaction equation of positive and negative electrodes is as follows:
(1)
Positive eletrode:
VO
2 +
+ H

⇌ Discharge Charge

(2)

When the battery is charged, V3+ gains electrons to V2+ at the negative electrode, while VO2+ loses electrons to VO2 + at the positive electrode. The resulting H+ enters the cathode electrolyte through the proton exchange membrane, thus maintaining the circuit conduction of the whole system and the charge balance of the electrolyte [25]. Compared with other energy storage devices, the VRFB has many outstanding features. (1) Battery output power and capacity can be designed flexibly. The power output can be achieved by increasing or decreasing the number of battery packs. The capacity is determined by the concentration of vanadium ions and the volume of electrolyte. Therefore, the scale of VRFB can be flexibly designed according to the actual demand. (2) Long cycle life. The positive and negative electrolytes of battery are placed in two liquid storage tanks respectively. The electrodes are made of corrosion-resistant materials with good stability and a cycle life of more than 10,000 times. (3) VRFB also has the advantages of short response time, high energy conversion rate, low cost, safety and environmental protection [26,27]. Just because VRFB has many advantages, it is widely used in many occasions such as energy storage equipment, power grid peak regulation, new energy vehicles, wind power generation, emergency power supply, etc [28,29].

However, VRFB also has some problems such as low battery energy density, large size, poor electrochemical activity of the electrode, etc [30]. Electrode is core component whose performance directly affects the overall performance of battery. Therefore, a lot of research has been done on the electrode to improve the performance of VRFB. Carbon-based materials mainly include carbon felt (CF), graphite felt (GF), and carbon paper (CP), which have large specific surface area, high stability and good electrical conductivity, and are the most widely used electrode materials in VRFB [31]. However, due to the lack of electrochemical activity and poor hydrophilicity of carbon-based materials, they will be modified properly before use. The commonly used modification methods include surface oxidation activation, surface etching, heteroatomic doping, and catalyst introduction. Surface oxidative activation is the introduction of oxygen-containing groups on the electrode surface through heat treatment, acid treatment, electrochemical treatment, oxygen plasma treatment, microwave treatment, etc. The obtained electrodes have higher hydrophilicity and more active centers [32–34]. Etching methods include acid etching, alkali etching, metal oxide etching and so on. As a result, porous structures, defects and nanorods are formed on the surface of the carbon electrode, which is beneficial to the adsorption and mass transfer of the electrolyte on the electrode surface [35–37]. Heteroatom doping is the doping of N, S, P, and other non-metallic elements into the carbon framework to change the charge distribution on the electrode surface [38,39]. Metals, metal oxides, and carbon-based materials are common catalysts for VRFB. Electrodes modified with metals and metal oxides can improve charge transfer rate, active center, and hydrophilicity of the electrode surface. The commonly used metals and metal oxides include Ir, Pt, Bi2O3, Mn3O4, TiO2, etc [40–45]. The electrode modified with carbon-based catalyst is widely used in VRFB due to its large specific surface area and high electrical conductivity. Common carbon-based catalysts include carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and biomass carbon. However, pristine carbon-based catalysts also have the problems of insufficient active centers and poor hydrophilicity, which limit their catalytic activity. Therefore, a variety of modification methods such as branched functional groups, heteroatom doping and composite with other materials have been proposed. Modified carbon-based catalysts make up for the deficiency of pristine catalysts and enhance the comprehensive performance of electrode. In this paper, the latest progress of carbon-based materials as VRFB electrocatalysts is reviewed, and their electrochemical properties and catalytic mechanism is discussed. Finally, application prospects and challenges of carbon-based catalysts in VRFB are discussed to provide guidance for future development.

The pristine CNTs, graphene and other carbon-based catalysts have limited catalytic performance. In order to improve the catalytic capacity of carbon-based catalysts themselves, researchers adopted different intrinsic treatment methods to introduce oxygen-containing groups or dope non-metallic elements on the catalyst surface [46–48]. Therefore, before introducing various carbon-based catalysts and their modification methods, it is necessary to clarify the catalytic mechanism of catalysts after intrinsic treatment.

Introduction of oxygen-containing groups proved to be an effective method to enhance the hydrophilicity and reactive sites of catalysts. –OH and –COOH are the two most common oxygen-containing groups, which researchers grafted onto the catalyst surface by methods such as heat treatment and acid treatment. The formed polar C–O bond increases adsorption of vanadium ions and promotes mass transfer, thereby catalyzing redox reaction [49,50]. The polarity and number of oxygen groups determine redox activity of vanadium ions. However, catalytic activities of –OH and –COOH are limited due to the relatively low polarity of O in the C–O bond and excessive introduction of it affects stability of carbon materials. To this end, researchers developed oxygen-containing groups such as –SO3H, -BO3H, and –CONHOH to replace –OH and –COOH. Experimental results confirm that the groups with higher polarity and O content further accelerate redox reaction and improve electrochemical stability [51–53].

The catalytic mechanism of various oxygen functional groups (-COOH, –OH, –SO3H, etc.) for vanadium ion redox reaction is shown in Fig. 2 a. The -B-H represents a certain oxygen group. When the battery is charged, VO2+ in the electrolyte is adsorbed to the electrode surface and exchanged with hydrogen ions in oxygen functional groups on the electrode surface, so that VO2+ is combined with B–O bond on the electrode surface. Then the electron is transferred to the electrode along the C–B–V bond, and the oxygen atom is transferred at the same time. Finally, the generated VO2 + is exchanged with the hydrogen ion in the electrolyte and returned to the electrolyte. The process from V3+ to V2+ is similar (Fig. 2b). First, V3+ is transferred from the electrolyte to the electrode surface and exchanged with the hydrogen ion on the oxygen functional group. The electron is transferred to the electrode surface along the C–B–V bond and combine with V3+ to generate V2+. Finally, V2+ exchanges with the hydrogen ions in the electrolyte to complete the reaction. The discharge process is opposite. Compared with the untreated carbon-based catalysts, oxygen-containing catalysts show faster electron transfer rates and oxygen transfer rates. In general, carbon-based catalysts grafted with oxygen functional groups have large specific surface area, high conductivity, abundant active sites, and good hydrophilicity, which comprehensively improves catalytic performance for active substances.

Besides introducing oxygen groups on the surface of carbon-based catalysts, heteroatom doping is also an effective method to improve the catalytic performance. Researchers doped N, P, S, and B into the carbon framework by chemical vapor deposition, ammonia oxidation, and impregnation-carbonization. Heteroatoms are covalently bonded to adjacent carbon atoms in the carbon backbone, causing larger asymmetric spin and charge densities, and altering the electronic structure and physicochemical properties of carbon materials [54–56]. As for the N element, strong electronegativity of N can facilitate mass transfer of active species by changing charge distribution. Doping into the carbon framework forms C–N–C as a reaction site, and its lone pair electrons can form an N–V transition state with V to enhance electron transfer kinetics. In addition, introduction of N into the graphitic carbon structure does not change the sp2 hybridization of C atoms, which provides delocalized electrons to improve conductivity of carbon materials. Elements such as B, S, and P have also been proved to be excellent dopants, and the catalytic mechanism is similar to that of N.

The catalytic mechanism of hetero-atom doped carbon-based catalyst for positive electrode reaction is shown in Fig. 2c. The M represents a certain nonmetallic element. For the charging process, first the lone pair electrons of M atom combine with VO2+ to form an M − V bond, which is reaction rate determining step. With positive shift of potential, the electrons enter antibonding orbital of VO2+ through M − V bond, and coordinate with water molecules to generate VO2 + and release 2 H+ at the same time. Finally, VO2 + is detached from active site back into the electrolyte. Catalytic mechanism of negative electrode reaction is similar to that of positive electrode (Fig. 2d). The lone pair of electrons of M atom combines with V3+ to form an M − V bond. As the potential shifts negatively, electrons enter anti-bonding orbital of V3+ through M − V bond and generate V2+. Finally, V2+ is detached from active site back into electrolyte. Discharging is the reverse process of charging. During whole process, M atoms only serve as active sites and are not consumed before and after reaction.

In addition to monatomic doping, diatomic doping such as N and P co-doping, N and S co-doping, and B and N co-doping have also been widely studied by researchers. In addition to heteroatoms entering carbon framework and bonding with C, covalent bonds such as P–N, S–N, and B–N can be formed between two heteroatoms. The synergistic effect of co-doped heteroatoms showed enhanced electrochemical activity of oxygen reduction reaction [38,39,57]. In short, the introduction of nonmetallic elements provides more active centers for the electrode. Combined with the large specific surface area and high conductivity of carbon-based catalysts, they jointly catalyze electrode reaction.

The carbon electrode of VRFB has poor wettability and insufficient active centers. Therefore, in order to improve electrochemical performance of electrode, researchers introduced different types of electrocatalysts on electrode surface. Carbon-based catalysts have become ideal materials for large-scale commercial applications because of their advantages such as easy access to raw materials, low cost, high conductivity, good stability, and easy functionalization. Carbon materials used in catalysts usually have nano size and porous structure, so they have large specific surface area, which can provide a lot of reaction places for active substances. However, the catalytic performance of untreated carbon-based catalysts is limited, so researchers employed different modification methods to improve the catalytic activity. For example, oxygen groups or non-metallic elements are introduced through heat treatment, acid treatment and vulcanization to increase the accessibility and reaction center of the catalyst. Metals or metal oxides are introduced by hydrothermal and electrodeposition to enhance the charge transfer process and mass transfer kinetics. In this part, the carbon-based catalysts, including fullerenes, carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), graphene, biomass carbon, carbon-carbon composite, and other carbon materials, are introduced. Various modification methods of carbon-based catalysts were discussed in detail, and catalytic activities and reaction mechanisms of different modified catalysts for redox reaction of vanadium ions were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fullerene C76 and carbon dots electrocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fullerenes and CDs are commonly used zero-dimensional carbon materials in the field of electrochemistry. Fullerenes are hollow molecules composed entirely of carbon and similar in structure to graphite, so they have good chemical stability, large specific surface area and high electrical conductivity. And the electron cloud around the fullerene molecule is conducive to the adsorption of ions [58–60]. CDs are spherical nanoparticles composed of crystalline carbon cores and amorphous carbon, generally less than 10 nm in size. With simple preparation, low cost, large specific surface area and abundant defect sites, CDs have broad application prospects in the fields of catalysis and energy storage [61,62]. Recently, these two carbon materials were used as catalysts for VRFB and exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity.

Allam et al. [63] applied fullerene to VRFB for the first time and explored the catalytic performance of fullerene C76 as VO2 +/VO2+ catalyst. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a and inset of Fig. 3b that each C76 molecule has a spherical structure with a size of less than 1 nm. C76 is dominated by sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon and contains a small amount of –OH and –COOH, which guarantees the sample with high electrical conductivity and abundant active centers (Fig. 3b). FI-IR and Raman further confirmed the existence of various groups and many defective structures in C76 (Fig. 3c and d). Fig. 3e and f demonstrate that C76 electrode has good catalytic performance and cycling stability for VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction. This can be attributed to the introduction of C76 to increase the adsorption capacity and reactivity of the electrode, accelerating the diffusion of active species and the electron transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface during the reaction. The same catalytic performance was shown by the incorporation of C76 into the heat-treated carbon cloth (TCC) and untreated carbon cloth (UCC), so C76 can be directly doped into the carbon cloth without the need to treat the carbon cloth. At the same time, the author also confirmed that the presence of C76 inhibited the chlorine precipitation in the mixed electrolyte.

Xi et al. [64] synthesized CDs modified graphite felt (CDs/GF) electrode by one-pot solvothermal method (Fig. 4 a). The red emission in Fig. 4b indicates that CDs is uniformly attached to GF surface. And the introduction of CDs does not change the morphology of GF (Fig. 4c and d). Compared to the ordered structure of the original GF (Fig. 4e), CDs/GF surface is disordered due to the dense growth of CDs on the GF surface with a 0.21 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 4f). Fig. 4g shows that the introduction of CDs greatly improves the hydrophilicity of GF. The battery can operate perfectly at high current density (350 mA cm−2), and energy efficiency (EE) remains at 74% after 1000 cycles at 150 mA cm−2. Furthermore, EE of CDs/GF electrode is significantly higher than that of original GF electrode at -20-90 °C (Fig. 4h). The introduction of CDs on GF surface is a method worthy of promotion. The CDs/GF electrode contains rich defect structures and shows excellent adsorption of active substances. CDs/GF electrode has excellent catalytic performance and high stability at different temperatures.

CNTs are considered the curl of graphene sheets, so single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) can be divided according to the number of graphene sheets. CNTs are very popular VRFB catalysts due to their low cost, high specific surface area, and excellent stability. Li et al. [65] applied pristine CNTs in VRFB for the first time and showed excellent performance, after which researchers carried out a lot of research. The pristine carbon nanotubes have few active centers, insufficient hydrophilicity, and poor bonding with carbon-based electrodes. Grafting functional groups, doping with non-metallic elements, and compounding with metal oxides have been proved to be effective methods to enhance the catalytic activity of CNTs.

Yan et al. [66] studied the catalytic performance of SWCNTs as catalysts for the positive and negative reactions of VRFB. To increase the oxygen content and defect structure, they treated pristine SWCNT (P-SWCNT) in H2SO4/HNO3 to obtain acidified SWCNTs (M-SWCNT). Acid treatment opened the ends of P-SWCNT and increased dispersion, leading to more active centers and specific surface areas of M-SWCNT. Experimental results confirmed that the M-SWCNT only exhibited catalytic activity towards the anode. The authors believe that the enhanced electrochemical activity of anode is attributed to the increase of oxygen functional groups of M-SWCNTs and surface defects caused by acid treatment, while the decrease of the electrochemical activity of the cathode is due to the decreased electrical conductivity of M-SWCNTs and hydrogen evolution reaction. However, follow-up studies have shown that the presence of oxygen groups and defects also has a good catalytic effect on the cathodic reaction. The reason for this phenomenon may be caused by excessive acid treatment.

Yan et al. [67] also reported the catalytic activity of original MWCNTs, hydroxyl MWCNTs, and carboxyl MWCNTs on VO2 +/VO2+ redox pairs of VRFB. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a and b that CF surface is covered by MWCNTs. Experiments show that carboxyl MWCNTs have the highest catalytic activity, which is due to the fact that the H+ in the carboxyl group is more easily replaced by V ions than the hydroxyl group. The catalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 5c. Carboxyl MCNTs have a large surface area, which increases the adsorption capacity of active species. Oxygen functional groups on MWCNTs increase electrode accessibility and active sites. However, the poor adhesion of carboxyl MWCNTs on CF resulted in poor electrode stability. After that, the same team [68] used Nafion solution to bond short carboxy multi walled carbon nanotubes to CF to obtain a uniform and firm modified electrode. Charge-discharge test by assembling the dynamic battery show good cycle stability. At 50 mA cm−2, EE of battery using short carboxyl MWCNTs is 5% higher than that of pristine battery (77.1%) and shows no decay after 50 cycles. However, the presence of binder may increase contact resistance and reduce inherent catalytic activity of the catalyst. Moreover, the cost of organic binders such as Nafion is high and there is a risk of polluting VRFB. Zhu et al. [69] generated pyrolytic carbon through in-situ pyrolysis of sucrose and used it as a binder. Pyrolytic carbon enhances the adhesion between GF and MWCNTs, and reduced contact resistance and electrochemical polarization. This method effectively avoids the problems of organic binder in VRFB. Liu et al. [70] studied the effect of other physicochemical properties on electrochemical performance of MWCNTs except for surface oxygen content by heat treatment and acid treatment. The results show that the VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction is more easily affected by the change of physical and chemical properties of MWNTs. Too long heat treatment time or too high acid treatment temperature can destroy the structure or change the conductivity of MWCNTs. The enhancement of electrochemical catalytic activity of the modified MWNTs is not only related to the surface oxygen content, but also related to the specific surface area, electrical conductivity, and unique structural changes. Sun et al. [49] obtained hydroxyl MWCNTs-modified GF, and further deepened the research on the catalytic mechanism of vanadium ion redox reaction. On the one hand, the presence of hydroxyl groups makes hydroxyl MWCNTs easier to load on the GF surface than pristine MWCNTs under the same conditions, which increases site of redox reactions. On the other hand, the hydroxyl MWCNTs have higher C–O bond content and larger negative charge density, which is favorable for the adsorption of active species. Different from previous studies, Rashid-Nadimi et al. [71] took MWCNTs modified CF as a whole and treated it with acid heat treatment (A-H-CNT/CF). The hydrophilicity and adsorption capacity of the modified electrode were significantly higher than that of the original CF. The calculation results show that the exchange current density of A-H-CNT/CF electrode is more than 100 times higher than that of CF electrode. In addition to heat treatment and acid treatment of MWCNTs, alkali treatment is also considered as a method of adding oxygen groups. He et al. [72] explored the treatment of CNTs by KOH at high temperature. The resulting CNTs possess many defects and oxygen functional groups, which increase the reaction kinetics of vanadium ions. At 30 mA cm−2, EE of battery using KOH-activated CNTs can reach 85.7% which is 4.4% higher than that of blank battery.

Introduction of different groups can also significantly enhance the catalytic performance of CNTs. Kwon et al. studied the catalytic performance of hydroxamic acid functionalized carbon nanotube (HAA-CNT) (Fig. 5d) [52], phosphate functionalized carbon nanotube (POH-CNT) (Fig. 5g) [73], borate-group functionalized carbon nanotube (Borate-CNT) (Fig. 5i) [53]. Fig. 5e, f, 5h, and 5j are diagrams of the catalytic mechanism of the reaction of different groups on the electrode. All three groups provide active sites for electrode reactions. Phosphate group and HAA group have strong chelation ability, and interact with vanadium ion to promote the electrode reaction. Catalytic effect of phosphate group and HAA group on VO2 +/VO2+ reaction is more obvious, which may be because there are more active centers. The different electronegativity between boron and oxygen in borate-group enhances the adsorption of active substances. In addition, the chemical corrosion of electrode materials in electrolyte is inhibited. Wang et al. [51,74] prepared sulfonated CNTs by hydrothermal method and explored the catalytic performance for positive and negative reactions. Fig. 5k–n is the electron microscope of original CNTs and sulfonated CNTs. Sulfonation treatment has no obvious effect on the morphology of CNTs. The catalytic mechanism of V2+/V3+ reaction is shown in Fig. 5o. Sulfonic groups enhance the adsorption of vanadium ions on the electrode and provide reaction sites.

The doping of heteroatoms such as N, S and P is an effective way to further improve the performance of CNTs. Manthiram et al. [75] and Yu et al. [76] prepared N-doped CNTs (N-CNTs) by chemical vapor deposition. The N element can adjust the electron distribution and surface polarity, and increase the surface defect structure and active centers of CNTs. During the redox reaction, the adsorption amount of vanadium ions is increased and the activation energy of the reaction is reduced, thereby improving the catalytic activity. However, the direct growth of CNTs on carbon-based electrodes by chemical vapor deposition is complicated and difficult to achieve. Catalysts such as ferrocene may not be completely removed in the acid wash resulting in contamination of VRFB. Hong et al. [54] obtained nitrogen-doped CNTs (N-CNTs) with different densities on the GF surface by pyrolyzing different types of metal (Fe, Co, and Ni) phthalocyanines (Fig. 6 a–d). The results show that Fe phthalocyanine as the precursor produces the most N-CNTs and has the best catalytic performance for redox reaction. This is a simple and effective method to prepare N-CNTs, but the formation mechanism of CNTs is not clear. In addition, pickling may not completely remove the metal impurities coated in CNTs. Sun et al. [77] reported the preparation of N-doped carboxyl MWCNTs using dopamine as the nitrogen source. Dopamine is non-toxic and an environmentally friendly natural nitrogen source. The synergistic effect of oxygen functional groups and N element doped into the carbon framework is the reason for the enhanced catalytic activity of MWCNTs. N-doped carboxyl MWCNTs reduces charge transfer resistance and increases the reversibility of the reaction. The EE of battery and the durability of electrodes are significantly improved. P element proved to be another effective dopant besides N. P doping into the carbon network can improve the electron contribution rate in graphitic carbon catalysts and the lone pair electrons of P are beneficial to the generation of P–V bond. He et al. [55] synthesized phosphorus-doped MWCNTs (Fig. 6e). The catalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 6f. P doping improves the charge distribution, and the lone pair of electrons of the P atom increases the number of reaction sites. As a positive electrode reaction catalyst, EE of battery is significantly improved. The catalytic performance of CNTs doped with two heteroatoms is better than that doped with one heteroatom. The same team [78] prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CNTs (MWCNTs-NS) using thiourea as the source of nitrogen and sulfur. MWCNTs-NS reduces the charge transfer resistance of the reaction and reduces the electrochemical polarization of battery. The synergistic effect of co-doped heteroatoms is conducive to the enhancement of electrochemical activity.

Precious metals have excellent conductivity and stability in strong acid environment. Transition metal oxides have strong hydrophilicity and can be used as active sites for positive and negative redox reactions. Therefore, the combination of CNTs with metals or metal compounds is another way to improve the catalytic performance of CNTs. Shieu et al. [79] modified GF by uniformly distributing the precious metal platinum (Pt) on MCNTs. The particle size of Pt is 2.7 nm ± 0.8 nm. Compared with commercially available Pt particles, self-made Pt particles have higher catalytic activity. However, the high price of Pt is not conducive to practical application. Therefore, researchers have developed some base metal compound catalysts. Wang et al. [80] prepared Mn3O4/MWCNTs composites by hydrothermal method and explored the catalytic activity of the positive electrode reaction of VRFB. Fig. 6g shows that the oxidized MWCNTs are uniformly distributed. It can be seen from Fig. 6h–j that with the increase of processing time, the content of Mn3O4 in Mn3O4/MWCNTs increases. The preparation process of Mn3O4/MWCNTs composites is shown in Fig. 6k. The catalytic activity of Mn3O4/MWCNTs treated for 7 h is the highest. The reason for the increased catalytic activity is that MCNTs acts as a bridge connecting Mn3O4 crystals. MCNTs and Mn3O4 form an effective conductive network, which accelerates the electron transfer and the diffusion of active substances in the reaction process. Faraji et al. [81] developed tungsten trioxide-multi walled carbon nanotubes (WO3-MWCNTs) as an electrocatalyst for the VO2 +/VO2+redox reaction by electrochemical deposition. The electrocatalytic kinetics of redox reaction is WO3-MWCNTs/graphite > MWCNTs/graphite > WO3/graphite > graphite. WO3-MWCNTs are interleaved on the surface of graphite, forming nanopore structures with large surface area and large porosity, providing reaction sites and active sites for positive electrode reactions. Thus, the efficiency is improved and the overpotential and internal resistance are reduced. Chu et al. [82] prepared Bi2O3/MWCNTs by liquid phase deposition-calcinating method (Fig. 6l and m), which showed good catalytic performance for the V2+/V3+ reaction. Electrochemical tests show that the charge transfer and ion diffusion rates on Bi2O3/CNTs are significantly higher than those on CNTs. In addition, XRD test shows that there are Bi species in Bi2O3/CNTs, which may be due to the reduction of Bi2O3 by carbon during calcination. The existence of Bi is beneficial to inhibit the hydrogen evolution reaction. Unfortunately, the mechanism of Bi inhibiting hydrogen evolution is not clear. Kwon et al. [83] prepared bismuth sulfate modified acidified CNTs (BiOS-ACNT) (Fig. 6n) with sulfuric acid. Its oxygen enriched functional groups provide active centers for positive and negative reactions. Fig. 6o shows the catalytic mechanism of BiOS-ACNT for electrode reaction. Compared with acidified CNTs without BiOS modification, BiOS-ACNT has faster electron transfer rate and diffusion rate. He et al. [84] reported the catalytic performance of SnO2-CNT composites for electrode reaction. At 150 mA cm−2 current density, EE of the modified battery is 7.2% higher than that of the blank battery. CNTs modified by metal compounds are a kind of materials with excellent catalytic ability. In short, CNTs modified by different materials are a promising catalyst. The synergistic effect of three-dimensional porous CNTs with high conductivity and metal compounds accelerate the electrode reaction.

CNFs are widely used in VRFB due to their high specific surface area, excellent corrosion resistance and electrical conductivity. Compared with GF, CNFs have smaller fiber diameters and can provide more reaction places for vanadium ion redox reaction. CNFs are often used as electrode materials in most studies. However, CNFs are brittle and easy to fracture, so they are difficult to be applied on a large scale. Therefore, it may be a reasonable way to support CNFs as catalysts on the surface of conventional carbon-based electrodes (such as GF, etc.).

Liu et al. [85] obtained CNF modified GF (CNGF) by chemical vapor deposition and used it in positive electrode reaction. This is also the first time CNFs has been used as a catalyst. Fig. 7 a and b are SEM images of CNGF. CNFs can perfectly combine with GF and evenly distribute on the surface, and curled CNFs make surface of GF become rough and porous. The diameter of CNFs is 100–200 nm, and no lattice fringes are found in HRTEM, indicating that the crystallinity of CNFs is very poor (Fig. 7c and d). The specific surface area of CNGF is 32.4 m2 g−1, which is ∼100 times higher than that of naked GF (Fig. 7e). This is consistent with the electron microscope results. The larger specific surface area increases the electrode reaction area. CV results confirm that CNGF has higher peak current and peak potential difference (Fig. 7f). And it can be seen from Fig. 7g that CNGF has higher discharge capacity and capacity retention rate. Fig. 7h shows the catalytic mechanism of CNGF on the positive electrode reaction. The larger specific surface area of CNGF provides more places for electrode reaction, and a large amount of CC increases the reaction sites. In conclusion, CNFs loading on GF significantly improves the performance of VRFB, making it an effective catalyst. The same group [86] also obtained two B and N co-doped CNFs and used them as cathode reaction catalysts. One is the separate doping of B and N into CNFs (B–CNF–N), which has a significantly higher catalytic activity than either B or N single-doped CNFs. The other is B, N bonding doping into CNFs (BN–CNF), but its catalytic performance is significantly degraded and inferior to either of the single doped ones. The lone pair electrons of N can provide reaction centers for vanadium ions. While B doping increases the number of hole-type carriers and forms C–B–C bonds that activate Π electrons, thereby accelerating charge transfer. Therefore, B and N elements in B–CNF–N play their respective roles to jointly catalyze the redox reaction. However, PN junctions may be formed during the preparation of BN-CNF, which significantly reduces the electrical conductivity of the material.

Graphene is a sp2 hybrid two-dimensional sheet structure in which carbon atoms are arranged into a hexagonal lattice. This unique structure makes it highly conductive and has a high specific surface area, and its edges or defects can provide the active site for the reaction. Graphene-based materials have been widely used in battery electrode materials due to their excellent electrical properties [87–90].

Ding et al. [91] proposed using solution coating to deposit graphene on the surface of CF as the positive electrode of VRFB (Fig. 8 a). The surface of CF is covered with graphene (Fig. 8b and c). Electrochemical tests show that compared with the original CF, Voltage efficiency (VE) and EE of this material are significantly improved, and the electrochemical polarization phenomenon is reduced. At the same time, the author found that after the fifth impregnation in graphene/Nafion solution has the highest catalytic activity for the VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction. The fabrication cost is reduced by a simple coating technique, which is beneficial to mass production. However, pristine graphene has insufficient active sites, so it is necessary to perform modification treatment before coating. And the introduction of Nafion solution leads to the decrease of catalytic activity. Cui et al. [92] reported the preparation of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets (GONPs) using an improved Hummers process (Fig. 8d). Compared with the original graphene, due to the introduction of oxygen-containing groups, GONPs show a good catalytic ability for V2+/V3+ redox reaction. Especially the samples processed under 50 °C and vacuum environment have the best catalytic performance for VO2 +/VO2+ and V2+/V3+ redox reactions. This is because the content of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups is the highest currently. When the temperature continues to rise to 120 °C, GONPs has the best electrochemical reversibility, which may be because catalyst has better conductivity at higher temperature. Blanco et al. [93] prepared carbon nanowalls (CNWs) thin films on gold substrates by radio frequency plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (RF-PECVD). They are two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanomaterials composed of self-aligned graphene nanodomains vertically arranged on the substrate. This structure helps electrons transfer from the substrate and leaves many exposed graphite edge planes with oxidized functional groups, which accelerates the positive reaction of VRFB. However, this method requires unique equipment and complex process, which increases the preparation cost.

In order to improve the catalytic performance of GO, heat-treated GO was proposed by the researchers and applied in VRFB as an active electrocatalyst. GO is usually obtained by the Hummers' method, making it possess more oxygen groups than graphene. However, the 2D graphitic structure of graphene is destroyed, resulting in a significant decrease in the conductivity of the resulting GO. Therefore, the researchers propose to heat-treat the obtained GO to restore the original sp2 domains of graphene to enhance the electrical conductivity. The GO after heat treatment still has residual oxygen groups, which can be used as active sites to catalyze redox reactions [50,94]. Blanco et al. [94] obtained thermally reduced graphene oxide (TRGO) at 1000 °C and inert atmosphere. The material was applied to the positive electrode reaction of VRFB. TRGO shows high conductivity and low overpotential for VO2 +/VO2+ redox reactions. High VE and EE were obtained in the charge-discharge experiment. Blasi et al. [50] proposed the synthesis of graphene oxide (TT-GOs) in a reducing environment and at different temperatures. Then, TT-GOs were distributed on the surface of CF by wet impregnation method, to study the catalytic activity of the electrode reaction. There are carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of TT-GOs, which is conducive to the redox reaction. The sample treated at 100 °C (TT-GO-100) has the highest catalytic activity and reaction kinetics for the cathode reaction, and the anode reaction is highly reversible. This is mainly attributed to the higher graphitized structure of the TRGO-100 sample than GO, and the ubiquitous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the sample.

Yan et al. [95] reported that GO was reduced by electrochemical methods to improve its catalytic activity. They obtained electrochemically reduced graphene oxide (ERGO) with different C/O ratios in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and under different negative potential conditions. Then study the catalytic activity of the material on the electrode reaction. From Fig. 8e–h, it can be observed that the corrugated and curled morphology of graphene nanosheets is very similar to a pleated silk veil. The inset shows that ERGO has a distinct graphitic crystal structure at higher negative potentials, which favors charge transfer. The Raman results show that the atomic disorder of ERGO is higher than that of GO, indicating the structural defects generated during the reduction process. CV tests confirmed that the catalytic performance for the cathode reaction was the best when the reduction potential was −1.4 V, and the anode reaction was most suitable when the reduction potential was less than −1.2 V. Too high a negative potential can lead to severe oxygen or hydrogen evolution reactions. Authors believe that CO is the key to catalyze redox reaction of vanadium ions rather than C–O, and give the corresponding catalytic mechanism (Fig. 8i). However, subsequent studies have shown that C–O also plays an important role in accelerating the kinetics of the redox reaction. Furthermore, the number of oxygen functional groups will decrease due to the evolution of oxygen and hydrogen after a long cycle. Therefore, the stable existence of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the catalyst has an important impact on the cycling stability of battery. Wu et al. [96] prepared a composite electrode composed of reduced GO and graphite felt (rGO-GF) by hydrothermal method and applied it in the redox reaction of VRFB. The electrode fabrication process is shown in Fig. 8j. The rGO is closely deposited on the GF surface. Compared with the original GF electrode, rGO-GF has higher conductivity and stability. And the EE is improved under the condition of large current density (Fig. 8k). The good catalytic activity of heat-treated GO on VRFB is attributed to its unique crystal structure and the existence of oxygen functional groups which increase the electrical conductivity and enhance the active site for the reaction. The composite electrode obtained in this study can operate at a high current density of 300 mA cm−2, which is higher than all previous works on graphene in VRFB. It can be seen that the hydrothermal reduction method is an effective method to prepare rGO-GF composite electrodes. The performance of the electrode is higher than that obtained by the heat treatment method.

It is well known that treatment of GO by heating or electrochemical methods can improve the catalytic performance of the redox reaction of vanadium ions. This is attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the material. However, the increase of oxygen-containing groups will affect the electrical conductivity of material, and the oxygen groups are difficult to exist stably during long-term cycling of battery. Nonmetallic element doping is an effective way to solve this problem. Liu et al. [97] prepared nitrogen-doped graphene nanosheets (NGS) using urea as the nitrogen source and studied their catalytic activity for the positive electrode reaction. The N atoms do not reduce the conductivity of the material and can provide active sites for electrode reactions. The electrochemical test results show that the catalytic activity and reversibility of NGS is much higher than that of rGO. And the peak current of NGS did not decay after repeated CV tests, which means that NGS has excellent cycling stability. In addition, authors confirmed that quaternary nitrogen plays a major role in the VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction, but neglected the role of pyridine nitrogen. Sun et al. [98] used thiourea as a source of sulfur and nitrogen to prepare nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene (rGO-NS) as a catalyst for VRFB positive reaction. It can be seen from Fig. 9 a and b that rGO-NS successfully attaches to GF surface. EE of the battery using rGO-NS can reach 85.77% at 80 mA cm−2, and there is no decay after 50 cycles. The synergistic effect of N and S increases rGO conductivity and active centers, thus enhancing the kinetics and stability of cathodic redox reaction. Etesami et al. [99] prepared phosphorylated GO (Fig. 9c) and loaded on CF. Phosphorylated GO has a high negative charge density and many defective structures on its surface, which enhance the adsorption capacity and catalytic activity of active substance.

Transition metal oxides are widely used as electrode catalysts because of their good chemical reactivity. metal oxide/graphene oxide composites can combine their characteristics to catalyze electrode reaction. Walsh et al. [100] synthesized nitrogen-doped reduced-graphene oxide/Mn3O4 composites (N-rGO-Mn3O4). The synergistic effect of several substances accelerates the redox reaction of VO2+/VO2 +.Wang et al. [101] obtained 3D annealed WO3 nanowire/graphene sheet (3D annealed WO3 NWs/GS) foam (Fig. 9d) and used it as electrode catalyst. This 3D structure with a large number of micropores promotes the electrode reaction. The EE of modified GF electrode is about 10% higher than that of original GF. Bimetallic oxides have been proved to have higher catalytic performance than mono-metallic oxides. Wang et al. [102] prepared titanium niobium oxide-reduced Graphene Oxide (TiNb2O7-rGO) nanomaterials. TiNb2O7 nanoparticles are firmly embedded in rGO (Fig. 9e–h). Experimental results show that the EE of battery has almost no attenuation after 200 charges and discharges at the current density of 120 mA cm−2. It is necessary to continue the development of this bimetallic oxide/carbon composite. The same group [103] also synthesized MoO2-reduced graphene oxide (MoO2-rGO) composite. MoO2 can be evenly and firmly distributed on rGO. MoO2-rGO with oxygen vacancy, high conductivity, and oxygen group is an excellent catalyst for electrode reaction. Wang et al. [104] obtained a niobium oxide-niobium carbide-graphene oxide (NbO2/NbC/rGO) hybrid material by reflux-hydrothermal-carbonization and used it as an electrocatalyst for VRFB. NbO2/NbC is uniformly distributed on the surface of rGO instead of agglomerating together. Hybrid materials combine the properties of metal compounds and graphene, giving them abundant active centers, oxygen vacancies, large specific surface areas, and excellent electrical conductivity. At a current density of 80 mA cm−2, EE of NbO2/NbC/rGO battery is 32.8% higher than that of blank battery. The degree of performance improvement is higher than that of all graphene-based composite catalysts.

Although the application of 2D graphene in VRFB has achieved good results, it can be further improved by using three-dimensional (3D) graphene. It overcomes the limitations of 2D graphene, such as irreversible stacking due to strong Π - Π bond interaction and agglomeration of nanosheets, resulting in the reduction of active surface area. In addition to the inherent physical and chemical properties of 2D graphene nanosheets, 3D graphene also has 3D conductive network, mesoporous and microporous structure, and significant high specific surface area. Tang et al. [105] synthesized 3D graphene-nanowalls by in situ microwave plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (Fig. 9i). They then used the material to modify CF and investigated its catalytic activity for the positive electrode reaction of VRFB. This structure increases the effective area of the electrode and improves the electrode stability. The electrochemical test results show that the catalyst has good catalytic activity for the positive electrode reaction. However, the preparation process is complicated and the cost is too high. Jung et al. [106] synthesized 3D mesoporous graphene-modified carbon felt (MG-CF) by a simple self-assembly interaction method and investigated its catalytic activity. The electrode synthesis process is shown in Fig. 9j. Fig. 9k–n shows that mesoporous graphene (MG) is uniformly distributed on the CF surface and forms a wrinkled structure. Electrochemical tests show that when the MG content is 4% (4 wt%), it has the highest catalytic activity for the electrode reaction. An appropriate amount of MG is added to the surface of the CF, which increases the specific surface area of the electrode, provides active sites for the electrode reaction, and improves the conductivity. The catalytic mechanism of the VO2+/VO2 +couple redox reaction on the MG-CF electrode is as follows. Firstly, ion exchange occurs between VO2+ and hydrogen ions from –OH functional group on the surface of CF electrode. Then, VO2+ combines with the O ion from the C–O group and electrons are transferred through the C–O–V bond to complete the transition from VO2+ to VO2 +. Finally, VO2 + ion exchanges with hydrogen ions in the electrolyte and diffuses back to the electrolyte. The process from VO2 + to VO2+ is completed by the coordination of the –OH group and CC. The same group [107] also proposed the hydrothermal synthesis of 3D nitrogen-doped mesoporous graphene functionalized carbon felt (NMG-CF) as the electrode material of VRFB (Fig. 9o). The catalytic performance of this material is better than that of MG-CF. This may be because the introduction of the N element causes more defects on the electrode surface, which provides active sites for electrode reactions. At the same time, the configuration of N also plays an important catalytic role in the reaction.

Recently, carbon materials obtained from biomass have been proven to be high-quality catalysts for VRFB. Biomass, which is widely available, low cost, and environmentally friendly, generally requires only simple carbonization to obtain samples. The obtained carbon-based catalysts have high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and abundant heteroatoms, which are natural places for the redox reaction of vanadium ions [108–112].

Zein containing various amino acids is a low-cost nitrogen source. Cho et al. [108] coated corn protein on the surface of carbon black particles to prepare N-doped and oxygen-rich carbon-based catalyst (N-CB) (Fig. 10 a). And the mechanism of corn protein coating on carbon black nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 10b. The α-helix generates β-sheet by evaporation-induced self-assembly, which drives the self-assembly of zein. Zein is a micron-thick sheet-like structure, while carbon black nanoparticles are only a few tens of nanometers (Fig. 10c). The obtained N-CB uniformly coats the CF surface and each CB nanoparticle is aggregated on the functionalized graphitic layer, which makes N-CB have more structural defects than CB (Fig. 10d). Fig. 10e shows the catalytic mechanism of N-CB on VO2+/VO2 + and V2+/V3+. The good catalytic ability of N-CB is attributed to the oxygen functional groups and the doping of N, which provide the active site for the electrode reaction and increase the electrical conductivity. Wu et al. [111] prepared functional porous carbon (PC) by hydrothermal treatment of pomelo peel in phosphoric acid. PC contains abundant mesopores, N, P species, and oxygen-containing groups, which exhibit synergistic catalysis for the VO2+/VO2 + redox reaction (Fig. 10f). VE and EE of battery using PC are significantly improved, and the electrochemical polarization phenomenon is weakened. Wang et al. [109] prepared porous carbon (FDC-K) through hydrothermal treatment of fungus and KOH high-temperature pyrolysis. Then they loaded porous carbon on the surface of GF as an electrode to study its catalytic activity for VO2 +/VO2+ redox reactions. Fig. 10g shows a synthesis diagram of porous carbon. FDC-K exhibits good wettability, which facilitates the adsorption of active substances (Fig. 10h). CV test results show that FDC-K has better catalytic performance than fungus calcined without KOH (FDC-C) and hydrothermal carbon (FDC-H) (Fig. 10i). The charge-discharge test results show that the capacity retention rate of the battery is 15.4% higher than that of the original battery after 50 cycles with the electrode of FDC-K at the current density of 50 mA cm−2. The excellent catalytic performance of FDC-K is attributed to its porous structure with high specific surface area, functionalization of oxygen-containing groups, and good wettability.

Aravindan et al. [110] used mesoporous activated carbon obtained by high-temperature carbonization of coconut shell as a catalyst for the electrode reaction of VRFB. The electrochemical test results show that the catalyst has good electrocatalytic performance for V3+/V4+ pairs. This is attributed to the high specific surface area of the carbon material prepared from the coconut shell, which provides sufficient space for the reaction of vanadium ions in the electrolyte. Lisak et al. [112] synthesized activated carbon with a mesoporous rate of 91.9% and a high specific surface area by wood chips as raw materials. Compared with the original graphite plate electrode, the graphite plate electrode using this material has lower charge transfer resistance to VO2 +/VO2+ and V3+/V2+ pairs and better catalytic performance. At higher current density and 50 charge-discharge cycles, the modified electrode has higher efficiency.

Although biomass carbon electrocatalysts have excellent electrochemical activity, some of them contain various impurities that cannot be removed, which may contaminate the electrolyte. In addition, compared with CNTs and graphene, biomass carbon has diverse morphologies and elements, which increases the difficulty of elucidating the catalytic mechanism. Therefore, focusing on structure-function correlation is the key to the future development of biomass materials in the field of electrochemistry.

To further improve the catalytic activity of carbon-based catalysts for the redox reaction of vanadium ions, carbon-carbon composite electrocatalysts were developed by researchers. Different types of carbon composites can give play to their respective characteristics, and their synergistic effect makes their catalytic performance higher than that of a single carbon-based catalyst.

Cho et al. [113] prepared a carbon nanofiber/nanotube (CNF/CNT) composite catalyst grown on CF and studied its catalytic performance for the electrode reaction of VRFB. The material is synthesized by thermal decomposition of acetylene with nano-Ni as catalyst (Fig. 11 a). Fig. 11b confirms the successful adhesion of dense CNF/CNT on CF surface. Charge-discharge tests show that CNF/CNT has lower overpotential and higher charge-discharge rate capacity and capacity retention capacity. The CNF/CNT prepared at 700 °C has the best catalytic performance for the positive and negative reactions. The excellent electrocatalytic performance of CNF/CNT is attributed to defects caused by the exposed edge plane of CNF, which provides active sites for the reaction. CNTs composed of inert substrate graphene sheets have high electrical conductivity and corrosion resistance. Cui et al. [114] prepared composite materials of graphene oxide nanosheets/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (GO/MWCNTs) deposited on GCE by electrostatic spraying method. The preparation process of the electrode is shown in Fig. 11c. Compared with GO, GO/MWCNTs significantly improves the reversibility of VO2 +/VO2+redox couple and reduces electrochemical polarization. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on GO enhance the active site for the reaction. The addition of MWCNTs forms a hybrid conductive network, which significantly improves the GO conductivity. GO/MWCNTs has excellent catalytic performance for VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction.

Kwon et al. [115] synthesized N-doped carbon (m-NC), carbon nanotubes (m-NCNT) and their composites (m-NC@NCNT) through two metal organic skeletons (ZIF-8 and ZIF-67) ((Fig. 11d). The order of catalytic performance for positive reaction is: m-NC < m-NCNT < m-NC@NCNT. This is because m-NCNT and m-NC@NCNT contain a large amount of graphite nitrogen, which is conducive to the desorption of vanadium ions. And N doping provides the reaction site. Lin et al. [116] synthesized a three-dimensional (3D) nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide/carbon nanotube composite (NrGO/CNT) as an electrocatalyst for the VO2 +/VO2+ redox reaction by a high-temperature pyrolysis hydrogel precursor. NrGO-1000 is composed of graphene sheets with irregular distribution and forms a three-dimensional porous structure (Fig. 11e and f). Fig. 11g and h shows that CNTs are evenly distributed on the rGO surface and the porous three-dimensional structure was not destroyed. The unique structure of NrGO/CNT avoids the formation of CNT bundles and the accumulation of rGO. A proper amount of N doping provides an effective active site for the electrode reaction. The interaction between rGO and CNT makes the material have good stability.

In addition to traditional carbon-based catalysts such as CNTs, graphene, and biomass carbon, other carbon materials from different sources or without specific structures can also exhibit good catalytic performance for vanadium ion redox reactions. Three-dimensional porous carbon materials have large specific surface area and high porosity, and are commonly used in the field of electrochemistry. Kim et al. [117] discussed the catalytic activity of mesoporous carbon (MC-Oxy) grafted with oxygen groups and interconnected pores for cathode reactions. The abundant mesopores are interconnected so that MC-Oxy has a large specific surface area (Fig. 12 a and b). Fig. 12c is a schematic diagram of MC-Oxy catalyzing the VO2+/VO2 + redox reaction. Vanadium ions are fully distributed in the pores of MC-Oxy, and oxygen groups increase the effective reaction area and active center.

At present, the preparation of carbon-based catalysts using polymers as precursors has been developed. Such catalysts usually have large specific surface areas, porous structures, and abundant heteroatoms. Shao et al. [118] prepared mesoporous carbon (MPC) by soft templating method using polymer as precursor, and then further processed in NH3 atmosphere to obtain nitrogen-doped mesoporous carbon (N-MPC). Nitrogen species lower the reaction energy barrier and the contact resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. And the pore size of N-MPC is only a few nanometers, which is beneficial to the transport of active species. Zhou et al. [47] used p-phenylenediamine and phytic acid as raw materials to obtain carbon network in situ modified GF (GF@CN) through cross-linking polymerization and carbonization (Fig. 12d). The CN supported on the GF surface provides a flow channel for the electrolyte (Fig. 12e). The presence of nitrogen and phosphorus species and oxygen groups in GF@CN reduces the activation energy for the redox reaction of vanadium ions (Fig. 12f). Compared with pristine GF electrode, GF@CN electrode accelerates the electron transfer rate and promotes the diffusion of active species. There is no degradation in the efficiency after 1000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 200 mA cm−2, which means that GF@CN electrode exhibits good cycling stability. Overall, heteroatom-doped carbon materials with abundant mesopores are a class of highly efficient electrocatalysts for VRFB. Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) is a promising precursor material with continuous microporous network. Kim et al. [119] treated PIMs with vapor induced phase separation technology and prepared GF (PIMC@GF) modified by nitrogen-doped graded porous carbon layer. Although there is no binder, PIMC can distribute uniformly and stably on GF surface and form defect structure. This solves the problem of large ohm resistance caused by the binder. Furthermore, the hierarchical pore structure, good wettability, and nitrogen species of PIMCs reduce mass transfer resistance and enhance redox activity.

Most carbon materials/metal oxide composites can exhibit ideal catalytic activity. Zhang et al. [120] investigated the catalytic activity of CP-coated super activated carbon/WO3 composite catalyst for the redox reaction of vanadium ions. Activated carbon has high specific surface area and electrical conductivity, but its active sites are insufficient. The existence of WO3 makes up for the shortcomings of activated carbon in this regard. The experimental results show that the super activated carbon/WO3 composite catalyst exhibits excellent catalytic performance for both positive and negative redox reactions. Lee et al. [121] prepared mesoporous foam carbon/MoO2 composite material and used it as VRFB catalyst. Different from other metal oxides, MoO2 has the advantages of good conductivity, high stability, and high-volume capacity due to its specific structure. Mesoporous carbon foams contain interconnected pore structures and abundant oxygen groups. The synergistic effect of the two makes it a composite catalyst with excellent catalytic activity.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have become popular precursor materials due to their large surface area, controllable structure, and functional modification. Su et al. [122] obtained a Co, N co-doped 3D nanocarbon-modified CF electrode (Co–CN@CF) using ZIF-67 as a precursor. ZIF-67 is dodecahedral and uniformly distributed on the surface of CF, and the Co–CN structure obtained after carbonization hardly changes. The presence of Co and N changes the charge distribution on the surface of the three-dimensional nanocarbons and improves the adsorption capacity of active substances. He et al. [123] prepared in-situ modified GF of porous nanocomposites (ZrO2@C/GF) using UIO-66 as a precursor (Fig. 12g). ZrO2@C nanoparticles can be uniformly distributed on GF surface and their particle size is ∼60 nm (Fig. 12h). GF electrodes modified with ZrO2@C showed higher EE at different current densities than previously reported for metal oxides. This study perfectly solves the problems of easy agglomeration and poor bonding of metal oxides on the surface of carbon electrode. The combination of MOF as precursor and in situ growth is a new method for the development of high-performance catalysts, which is worthy of further study.

The application of VRFB provide people with an ideal energy storage device. However, the further development of vanadium batteries is limited by the problems of low energy density, large volume, and poor electrochemical activity of electrodes. Carbon-based catalysts have large specific surface area, good electrical conductivity, porous and rich active centers, which are an effective way to improve the catalytic activity of electrodes. Pristine carbon-based catalysts have poor hydrophilicity and insufficient active sites, so grafted oxygen groups, heteroatom doping, and compounding with metal compounds have been widely studied by scholars. This paper reviews the application of various carbon-based catalysts in VRFB, discusses the catalytic mechanism for the redox reaction of vanadium ions, and analyzes the advantages and disadvantages. In order to promote the application of carbon-based catalysts in VRFB, we put forward the following prospects based on the current research progress:
1.
The introduction of calculation provides theoretical support for research. Surface chemistry is the core of carbon-based catalyst design, but the catalytic mechanism of some modified carbon-based catalysts for vanadium ions is still unclear, which largely hinders the research progress of VRFB. Therefore, for different catalysts, the introduction of appropriate calculation methods can provide theoretical support for research. This helps researchers to explore the catalytic mechanism of materials for redox reactions, and to more accurately recognize the reason why the catalyst improves the battery performance, thereby accelerating the development of catalyst materials and further improving the comprehensive performance of VRFB.
2.
Cheap electrocatalysts and simple processes should be explored to reduce costs. The preparation process of some carbon-based electrocatalysts is complicated and the cost is high, so it is not suitable for large-scale production. Carbon materials prepared from low-cost biomass have been proved to have the advantages of high conductivity, large specific surface area, and containing heterogenous elements. In addition, the preparation process only needs simple carbonization in most cases. Therefore, the electrocatalyst with simple preparation process and high cost performance should be further explored.
3.
The modified electrode materials with stable structure are prepared to ensure the long-term operation of the battery. The firmness of the combination of catalyst and electrode material directly determines the long-term performance of the battery. In order to ensure that the catalyst has long-term and stable catalytic activity for vanadium ion redox reaction, in the process of introducing the catalyst into the electrode material, the overall structure of the material should be stable and the catalyst should be evenly distributed on the surface. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the preparation method of electrode with stable structure. The results show that in-situ growth of catalysts on electrode materials is an effective method and needs to be further developed.
4.
The electrode is used efficiently to improve the battery capacity. Carbon-based catalyst modified electrode has large specific surface area, but the utilization rate of electrode area is low. Therefore, it is necessary to explore appropriate methods to improve the utilization of electrode area. It is a wise method to introduce a reasonably designed flow field. In the past, no flow field batteries caused the electrode “dead zone” and the uneven distribution of active substances in the porous electrode. The traditional serpentine flow and staggered flow field can improve the above problems to some extent, but the pressure drop loss will be large and the mass transfer capacity is low. It is advisable to introduce a suitable physical model to explore the flow behavior of electrolyte, which is conducive to the study of flow field.
